title_en: Al-Hakim al-Naysaburi and the Concept of the Mustadrak
content_en:
Abu Abdallah Muhammad ibn Abdallah ibn Muhammad al-Hakim al-Naysaburi was born in 321 AH in Nishapur, the cultural and intellectual capital of the Khorasan region. He lived through one of the most productive periods in Islamic scholarship and became the leading hadith authority of his era in the eastern Islamic world. His knowledge of hadith chains of transmission was encyclopedic, and he served in judicial and administrative positions while simultaneously maintaining a prodigious output as a hadith scholar and teacher.

Al-Hakim studied under hundreds of teachers across the breadth of the Islamic world, making extensive journeys to Iraq, the Hijaz, and Syria in addition to his native Khorasan. Among his most celebrated teachers were the great Khorasani scholars of his generation, and he built up chains of transmission of exceptional variety and depth. His students included major figures of the subsequent generation, and his teaching circles in Nishapur became the most important center of hadith transmission in the eastern Islamic world during the late fourth century.

The concept behind the Mustadrak is captured in its full title: al-Mustadrak ala as-Sahihayn — literally, the supplement to the two Sahihs. Al-Hakim's project was to identify hadiths that met the authentication criteria of al-Bukhari and Muslim but had not been included in their collections. He argued that the two great masters had selected only a portion of the authenticated traditions available to them, and that a significant body of additional authenticated material meeting their standards had been left out. His aim was to recover this material and present it to the scholarly world as an authorized supplement.

Al-Hakim died in 405 AH. The Mustadrak he left behind has been the subject of sustained scholarly debate ever since, praised for the important traditions it preserves and criticized for the inconsistency of its authentication judgments — a debate that is itself one of the most instructive case studies available in the history of hadith criticism.
